instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: [UX] display more informative ellapsed time info in progress bar output for short tasks
Body: > [!WARNING] > This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines]( and in particular the sections for [new contributors]( and the ["Needs triage"]( label. Once fit tasks are completed, the progress bars display for tasks that last less than a second, which is quite common in practice. I think we should display milliseconds and maybe drop the leading for hours, since we almost never do fit tasks that last hours (at least in interactive shells and notebooks where progress bars are most useful). More concretely, if a fit task last 10 ms, we could display or for instance.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: DOC: inconsistent monotonic_cst docstring across forest estimators
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation ### Describe the issue In , the parameter docstring has two inconsistencies across the forest estimators: **1. Missing trailing comma in shape notation** All four classes ( , , , ) write the shape as: array-like of int of shape (n_features) It should be: array-like of int of shape (n_features,) This is inconsistent with the rest of the scikit-learn codebase which uses the trailing comma to denote a 1D tuple. **2. Inconsistent wording for constraint values** uses: - 1: monotonic increase - -1: monotonic decrease While , , and use: - 1: monotonically increasing - -1: monotonically decreasing Both should use the same phrasing. The adjective form ("monotonically increasing/decreasing") is more grammatically consistent with how constraints are described elsewhere. ### Steps to reproduce Open and search for . ### Suggested fix - Add trailing comma: in all four classes - Standardize wording to "monotonically increasing" / "monotonically decreasing" across all four classes ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix _No response_

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: BinaryTree/KDTree statistics are not thread-safe (and might be a performance issue)
Body: > [!WARNING] > This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines]( and in particular the sections for [new contributors]( and the ["Needs triage"]( label. ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact In , we have: and these are incremented in methods called by . Since a tree can be simultaneously used from multiple threads: 1. The statistics will be garbage, as the numbers will get overwritten by different threads. 2. It's possible that this will slow down operation as the same memory needs to be pushed between caches across CPU cores. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results Running in parallel should, given the current contract, give the same results. This is not really possible. So really the API should be changed so does _not_ reset to zero, and instead increments existing stats. ### Actual Results ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug I might fix this, depending. The lack of thread safety isn't a significant correctness issue; might be a performance issue though. was a previous attempt to remove these, stalled because it was unclear if performance was an issue. My plan: 1. Try to determine if this is a performance bottleneck. 2. If it is a bottleneck, try switching to thread-locals to calculate this, and then stats would be correct. **This will require a small API change:** stats will not reset to zero at the start of , but rather get incremented to existing stats. 4. If it's not a bottleneck, or the thread-local approach proves non-viable, or the small API change is unacceptable: deprecate the API, document that its results are garbage, and then in the future this can eventually be removed.

input:
Title: sklearn.callbacks._transport.open_listener should document thread-safety requirements
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation The callable argument to can be called from multiple threads within the same process, when using a thread pool instead of a process pool. That means it needs to be thread-safe. In practice all callers are actually thread-safe, so that's not a problem. However, this requirement is not documented. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix Add a note saying that the might be called from multiple different threads and must be prepared to handle this correctly. I'm happy to do this.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Handling of classes with all-zero weights
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact If passing multi-class data to a classifier where some class has only observations with zero weights, estimators will produce odd solutions which do not really make sense. For example: Mathematically, the correct solution would be something with a minus infinite as intercept and zeros in all coefficients, and the same applies to other classifiers with intercepts (e.g. gradient boosters), but I don't think this would be a desirable solution. Technically, the sample weights according to the documentation should be interpreted as if each observation would be repeated the amount of times given by the weights, and if we take that as zero, that means leaving them out, in which case the classes with zero weights would be removed during the pre-processing and would not be available under . But that sort of behavior would be quite problematic for other libraries that aim at providing scikit-learn-compatible interfaces - for example, it'd contradict the docs that instruct developers to call to get the classes ([link]( and if implemented in such a way that [validate_data]( could return something with different shape than the input, it might require other scikit-learn-compatible libraries to have a hard dependency on scikit-learn, and would likely break many things if the shapes differ. Thus, I think the most reasonable behavior here would be to error out with an informative message. For reference, this is what GLMNET would do, and would even have a non-zero threshold for it which is perhaps a more reasonable way of handling it: ### Steps/Code to Reproduce Reproducer given above. ### Expected Results In order of preference, should either: * Error out; or * Filter out the classes; or * Produce a mathematically correct solution. ### Actual Results Produces an incorrect solution. ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug No.

Title: Interactions between array API and sparse coefficients are not documented
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation Array API support was recently expanded to cover LogisticRegression, which allows having sparse coefficients: It's not expected that coefficients would have exact zeros with the supported solver 'lbfgs' for array API (although it can still happen when columns have the same value in all rows), but technically a user can still call on an estimator that was fitted to array API classes, in which case it will fail. The documentation is not clear about limitations of LogisticRegression under array API, such as not being able to sparsify coefficients or not being able to make predictions if it somehow succeeds. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix Documentation should be clear about limitations of methods from estimators when using array API classes.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Add a warning whenever is called with non-numeric values but no labels provided
Body: > [!WARNING] > This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines]( and in particular the sections for [new contributors]( and the ["Needs triage"]( label. ### Describe the workflow you want to enable Whenever an array-like of string types for a multi-class classification problem is passed into the function without an argument passed for labels, the confusion matrix is returned with the classes in alphabetical order. While this is expected behaviour and the natural way to sort anything string-like, the outputted confusion matrix may have an output order that is different from the user's expectations, especially if the classes are ordinal. Since the output of the function is an array, without any explicit labels, this means it will need to be manually re-aligned with the labels. There is currently no warning for this, possibly leading to misunderstood confusions matrices by users. ### Describe your proposed solution Near the start of the function, check if the type of the arrays are string-like (or another type without an inherent order) and if is , i.e. the default value. If this condition is True, raise a warning for the user that their confusion matrix will be returned in alphabetical order. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant There is a valid alternative view that the user should be aware their inputs have no natural order, unless provided, and so should take responsibility for the validity of their inputs. However, this proposed warning would not affect a small number of use cases and would induce a very small computational overhead ### Additional context _No response_

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Add 'quantile' strategy to
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable Choose between different binning strategies ("uniform" fixed width bins, "quantile" equal mass bins) to estimate the calibration curve. ### Describe your proposed solution Adding in a new or option to switch between the two binning strategies. We can for now just add the option and change the default in a follow up PR. We can also add (in the same or follow up PR) the option to implement the CORP approach as advocated in #23132. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context There are multiple issues / PRs on implementing calibration metrics (for example the expected calibration error, the Brier score decomposition) and improving reliability diagrams: #18268, #11096, #22233, #21774, #23767, #23132 There are two main ways to estimate the calibration curve (and metrics): 1. using binning, either "uniform" (fixed-width bins) or "quantile" (equal mass bins) 2. using isotonic regression, this is the [CORP]( approach, see #23132 For the binning case, the quality of the estimate in the binning depends crucially on 1a. the number of bins, it seems that is best, this is tackled by #33856 1b. the binning strategy, it seems that "quantile" is better, see [Roelofs et al]( or [Nixon et al]( Reading through the literature, I feel that the "best estimator for the calibration curve/metrics" is not yet settled. Based on the experiments reported in the [CORP]( paper, the isotonic regression seems the most accurate. However there are ways to debias the binned estimates for the Brier score decomposition that we could investigate, see [Ferro and Fricker, 2012]( Also, we might want the different options with each its own benefits: fixed-width bins to visually compare different curves or put them in a dataframe, equal mass bins to get a more reliable curve with a manageable length ( ), isotonic regression for its accuracy but at the expense of a crowded curve (length ). Overall, having the different options seems to me the best way for further implementations of the calibration metrics or curve, without having to commit to one option over another. cc @ogrisel @lorentzenchr

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: ENH: Add confusion_matrix_diff to compare multiple models against a baseline
Body: > [!WARNING] > This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines]( and in particular the sections for [new contributors]( and the ["Needs triage"]( label. ### Describe the workflow you want to enable When comparing classification models, users frequently need to understand how one model differs from another on a per-class basis. The current workflow requires manually computing separate confusion matrices and subtracting them: This pattern has no built-in support in sklearn and becomes error-prone when normalization is involved - normalizing before vs after subtraction gives different results and users may not be aware of this distinction. There is also no standard scalable way to compare multiple models against a single baseline in one call. ### Describe your proposed solution Add confusion_matrix_diff to sklearn.metrics with the following signature: **Label mode (precomputed=False):** **Precomputed mode (precomputed=True):** Returns a 3D array of shape (n_comparisons, n_classes, n_classes) where: - Positive values = comparison model better than baseline - Negative values = baseline better than comparison model The normalize parameter mirrors confusion_matrix exactly - normalization is applied to each matrix individually before subtraction, giving meaningful per-class rate differences rather than raw count differences. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant Users can subtract confusion matrices manually, but this does not handle: - Consistent normalization across matrices before subtraction - Multi-model comparison in a single call - Input validation for mismatched label sets or matrix shapes A utility function makes this discoverable, correct by default, and consistent with the existing confusion_matrix API. ### Additional context - No changes to existing functions — additions only - Placement: sklearn/metrics/_classification.py alongside confusion_matrix - Exported from sklearn.metrics - Full numpydoc docstring and tests included in the PR Happy to adjust the API design based on maintainer feedback before writing any code.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: DOC: Add explicit mathematical formula for biased standard deviation in StandardScaler
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation The API reference says it Standardize features by removing the mean and scaling to unit variance and mentions in the "Notes" section, but does not explicitly state a mathematical equation. For users with a statistical or mathematical background, it may be less transparent to rely on the documentation details of a third party library like NumPy than to provide a formal mathematical definition, which is easy to interpret as the difference between biased and unbiased estimators may not be immediately obvious to all users. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix I propose adding the explicit LaTeX formulas for the $z$-score and the standard deviation to the "Notes" section of the docstring. This clarifies the use of the biased estimator (division by $n$) versus the unbiased estimator (also known as Bessel's correction, $n-1$), which might be a point of confusion for users. I propose rewriting Notes as: We use a biased estimator for the standard deviation, equivalent to numpy.std(x, ddof=0) , that is: .. math:: s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2} I’d be happy to submit a PR if this approach looks good.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Support for building against a SciPy which uses ILP64 BLAS and
Body: Hi all, this issue is to propose adding support for building scikit-learn against a SciPy install that was built with ILP64 (i.e., 64-bit integers rather than the default LP64 / 32-bit integers - see ### Context SciPy has wanted to have solid/full ILP64 support for years (efforts going back to 2020, and feature requests before that), because there are use cases and algorithms where support for large arrays where array indices don't fit in plain 's is desirable. In the next SciPy release, 1.18.0, this will finally be arriving. When building from source, there will actually be three builld modes: 1. LP64-only (default) 2. ILP64-only (opt-in with ) 3. ILP64 for all SciPy internals, but LP64 for and low-level interfaces (opt-in with ) See for details. Wheels on PyPI will continue to be LP64-only, so the ABI of and won't be broken. However, when building from source, ILP64 can be chosen - and going ILP64-only means that downstream users of need to work with that change. Mode (3) can be useful as a transition mechanism for adopters (and wouldn't affect scikit-learn), but mode (2) is probably preferred. ### Proposed solution It is possible for scikit-learn and other downstream users to detect how SciPy was built, and adapt automatically - no new build flag will be needed in scikit-learn, it should "just work". Essentially, will expose a that can be used. It's also not too difficult to use that conditionally when it exists, and fall back to plain otherwise. Then, there are two ways scikit-learn can go for its own Cython code: 1. Handle the change in [sklearn/utils/_cython_blas.pyx]( with a small shim in each function, and keep [sklearn/utils/_cython_blas.pxd]( unchanged. 2. Change as well to use , meaning all consumers of that interface (~15 files currently) need to be adapted as well. It's not an either/or. Option 1 is a very localized change, and should probably be done either way. Option 2 can always be done later - it has the benefit of actually enabling use of large arrays with scikit-learn APIs as well (option 1 doesn't, because still uses ). So it depends on whether there's a benefit in doing that. ### Next step I plan to open a PR for (1) soon. This is fairly well-tested now ( and we are adding a test package to SciPy CI that closely mirrors scikit-learn's wrappers. Also, we now have a regression test for Cython ABI/signature changes, so that should be fine now. If you prefer to do (2), I'd be happy to work on that as well.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: [BUG] BayesianRidge.predict with return_std=True fails to center test features, leading to incorrect predictive variance
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact In , the predictive variance calculation in the method is mathematically inconsistent with the training process when (the default). During , the model centers the features ( ), but during , the variance is calculated using the **raw** test features instead of centering them by the training mean . As a result, the model reports its lowest uncertainty at the coordinate origin rather than at the center of the training data. For datasets far from the origin, this leads to significantly wrong uncertainty estimation, violating the principle that a Bayesian model should be most certain in the region where it has observed the most data. #### Mathematical formulation The original problem is $y = w^T x + b + \epsilon$. Instead, scikit-learn solves a centered version $y = w^T \tilde{x} + b' + \epsilon$, where $\tilde{x} = x - E[x]$, in order to find $w$ and $b'$, and finally $b$ is computed as $b = b' - w^T E[x]$. First, $b'$ is estimated using a frequentist approach (OLS/MLE) to be $b' = E[y]$, since $E[\tilde{x}] = 0$. This allows the problem to be reduced to $\tilde{y} = y - b' = w^T \tilde{x} + \epsilon$, on which a Bayesian approach is used to find $w$ (the posterior mean). After $w$ is found, the predictive distribution of $\tilde{y}$ on a new test point $\tilde{x}$ is then given by $$\tilde{\mu} = w^T \tilde{x}$$ $$\sigma^2 = \alpha^{-1} + \tilde{x}^T \Sigma_w \tilde{x}$$ But since $y = \tilde{y} + b'$, the predictive distribution of $y$ has the mean shifted to $$\mu = w^T \tilde{x} + b'$$ while the variance stays the same. However, our test point is actually $x$, so this all becomes $$\mu = w^T (x - \mu_x) + b' = w^T x + b$$ $$\sigma^2 = \alpha^{-1} + (x - \mu_x)^T \Sigma_w (x - \mu_x)$$ where $\mu_x = E[x]$ (estimated using the training data). Note that returns and , where is exactly $\mu$, but is not $\sigma$; instead it is $$\alpha^{-1} + x^T \Sigma_w x$$ What is missing is to subtract $\mu_x$ from $x$ before the variance computation. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results Lowest variance close to the training data. ### Actual Results Lowest variance close to the origin. Note that this is fine if the data is already centered; this is the problem scikit-learn is actually solving. ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug The bug is very easy to fix: just add a single line subtracting the mean before the variance calculation.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Hopefully Helpful (AI) Contributing Policy Proposal
Body: I saw a [video]( about the Open Source Community problems related to AI Contributing and I saw your [Issue]( related to that. In another video, there was also mention of a proposed solution that GitHub could even have developed. 2 months later, GitHub has still not developed it, and there was a video in recent days suggesting that it might actually be better that GitHub did not develop it. So I made a very simple GitHub Action: - If someone has a defined — configurable — permission on GitHub (for example admin/pull), then they can open a PR. - If someone has at least 1 open Issue assigned to them, they can open a PR. - If they do not meet either of the above conditions, then they receive a message to channel their energy to a place where there is a shortage of them. This is a bit different from your current 'You can open PRs to not Needs Triage Issues after commenting under them'. If you find it useful, then in your current flow I could imagine it as follows: - Either you allow an always open issue for an external contributor (for example a 'I am not an AI introduction: I would like to contribute to scikit-learn' issue) - Or you explicitly assign the issue to the person who indicated in a comment that they are working on it. (A human contributor takes far more time than this to complete it, and the AI contributor does not have a sense of time, it does not wait) Why I think my solution is better than the competing ones: Simple installation (you only need to copy+paste one file), transparent rules and It redirects AI contributors instead of blocking them — less likely to trigger a strong reaction. If I have piqued your interest, you can find it here:

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: ENH: Add FARC-HD (Fuzzy Association Rule-based Classification Model for High-Dimensional problems)
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable Currently, scikit-learn lacks native support for robust Fuzzy Logic-based classifiers, specifically those optimized for high-dimensional datasets. Researchers and practitioners often have to rely on external, outdated tools to run these types of algorithms, which breaks the standard Python data science workflow. I would like to propose the inclusion of the FARC-HD classifier to allow users to build fuzzy rule-based pipelines natively. ### Describe your proposed solution I have developed a fully functional Python implementation of the FARC-HD algorithm (Alcalá-Fdez et al., 2011). My current implementation closely follows the scikit-learn API, inheriting from BaseEstimator and ClassifierMixin, and processes standard NumPy arrays seamlessly. **Inclusion Criteria Check:** The algorithm meets the strict inclusion criteria for scikit-learn: - Age: The original paper was published in 2011. - Citations: The core paper "A Fuzzy Association Rule-Based Classification Model for High-Dimensional Problems with Genetic Rule Selection and Lateral Tuning" has over 470 citations on Google Scholar, far exceeding the 200+ threshold. - Relevance: It is a highly cited and well-established baseline for fuzzy classification in complex spaces. **Technical Architecture & Dependency Considerations:** Currently, my working prototype uses numba for JIT compilation to handle the heavy mathematical computations of the fuzzy logic rules efficiently. I am aware of scikit-learn's general preference to avoid heavy dependencies like Numba in the core library. If the core developers agree that FARC-HD is a valuable addition, I would be open to exploring the possibility of porting the performance-critical sections to Cython, should you deem it strictly necessary for integration. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant The main alternative is publishing this as an independent PyPI package or submitting it to scikit-learn-contrib (where Numba is generally accepted). While I am evaluating those routes, I believe FARC-HD's academic standing makes it a strong candidate for native inclusion in the core library. ### Additional context Would you be open to discussing the potential inclusion of this algorithm and the acceptable technical approach to handle the computational bottlenecks?

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Redundant sparse checks in linear_model/_base.py causing unnecessary repeated issparse() calls
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact There is a small redundancy in where is called multiple times on the same variables ( and ). The condition first checks: if sp.issparse(X) or sp.issparse(y): Then later, the same checks are repeated separately: if sp.issparse(X): ... if sp.issparse(y): This leads to unnecessary repeated function calls, which can be avoided by caching the results in boolean variables. Impact: Minor performance inefficiency and reduced code clarity. While it does not break functionality, it introduces avoidable redundancy in a frequently used code path. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results Sparse checks should be performed once and reused via cached boolean variables to avoid redundant calls. ### Actual Results The code runs correctly, but is called multiple times on the same variables, leading to minor inefficiency and less optimal code structure. ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug Yes, I would be interested in working on a PR. Root cause: Repeated calls to instead of caching results. Proposed fix: Cache results using: X_is_sparse = sp.issparse(X) y_is_sparse = sp.issparse(y) Then reuse these variables in all subsequent conditions. This improves readability and avoids redundant computation.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: [BUG] Partial dependence integer-feature deprecation message references outdated version (1.9)
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact ## Summary around line 720 still emits a stating that integer-valued feature inputs "will raise ValueError in scikit-learn 1.9". scikit-learn 1.9 has passed, so the warning message is stale and the behavior has not been updated to match the original intent. ## Current behavior Passing integer feature indices to emits: ## Expected behavior Either: 1. The behavior raises as originally promised (the deprecation endpoint is enforced), or 2. The deprecation period has been extended and the message is updated to reference the correct future version ## Files affected - (~line 720) - - Possibly one user guide page if it documents this warning ## Question for maintainers Was the ValueError enforcement intentionally deferred beyond 1.9? If so, what is the target version? I'd like to implement the fix once the intended end-state is confirmed — I don't want to enforce the error if the timeline was quietly extended. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce import numpy as np import warnings from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier from sklearn.inspection import partial_dependence X = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6], [7, 8]]) y = np.array([0, 1, 0, 1]) model = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=10, random_state=0) model.fit(X, y) with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as w: warnings.simplefilter("always") partial_dependence(model, X, features=[0]) for warning in w: print(warning.category.__name__, ":", warning.message) One of two consistent outcomes: 1. If the deprecation endpoint has been reached: a ValueError is raised for integer-valued feature inputs, as originally promised in the warning message. 2. If the deprecation period was extended: the FutureWarning message references the correct future version, not the already-passed 1.9. Either way, the current message should not reference a version that has already been released. A FutureWarning is emitted referencing scikit-learn 1.9, which has already been released: FutureWarning: ... will raise a ValueError in scikit-learn 1.9 The warning message is stale — scikit-learn 1.9 has passed but the behavior has not changed. Users following this warning will expect an error that never comes, eroding trust in the deprecation process. Relevant source location: - sklearn/inspection/_partial_dependence.py (~line 720) - sklearn/inspection/tests/test_partial_dependence.py shell **Versions** Note: the issue was identified by reading the main branch source directly. Installed version shown below for reference. System: python: 3.10.0 (tags/v3.10.0:b494f59, Oct 4 2021, 19:00:18) [MSC v.1929 64 bit (AMD64)] executable: C:\Users\tanmm\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python310\python.exe machine: Windows-10-10.0.26200-SP0 Python dependencies: sklearn: 1.7.2 pip: 21.2.3 setuptools: 57.4.0 numpy: 1.26.4 scipy: 1.15.3 Cython: None pandas: 2.3.3 matplotlib: 3.10.8 joblib: 1.5.3 threadpoolctl: 3.6.0 Built with OpenMP: True threadpoolctl info: user_api: openmp internal_api: openmp num_threads: 32 prefix: vcomp filepath: C:\Users\tanmm\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python310\Lib\site-packages\sklearn\.libs\vcomp140.dll version: None user_api: blas internal_api: openblas num_threads: 24 prefix: libopenblas filepath: C:\Users\tanmm\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python310\Lib\site-packages\numpy.libs\libopenblas64__v0.3.23-293-gc2f4bdbb-gcc_10_3_0- .dll version: 0.3.23.dev threading_layer: pthreads architecture: Prescott user_api: blas internal_api: openblas num_threads: 24 prefix: libscipy_openblas filepath: C:\Users\tanmm\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python310\Lib\site-packages\scipy.libs\libscipy_openblas- .dll version: 0.3.28 threading_layer: pthreads architecture: Haswell Yes — I would like to open a PR once maintainers clarify the intended end-state: - If ValueError enforcement is appropriate for main: I will update the behavior and adjust the tests accordingly. - If the deprecation was deferred: I will update the warning message to reference the correct target version. I will not open a PR until this is confirmed to avoid implementing the wrong end-state. Happy to be assigned once the approach is agreed. Root cause: sklearn/inspection/_partial_dependence.py ~line 720 contains a FutureWarning with a hard-coded version string that was never updated when the deprecation timeline changed or the removal was not enforced at 1.9.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Improve utils._indexing.py error messaging for missing columns
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable The ColumnTransformer lets you pick a subset of columns to apply transformers to (or to keep or drop). I find this immensely useful in my day to day work, since it makes complex featurization pipelines a lot more maintainable. One speedbump I keep running into is the error messaging when columns are missing. Let's dissect this example, where I make a dataframe and try to access two columns which are not present: Truncating the traceback a little bit to the relevant parts: Although the Pandas traceback catches the first missing column ("Missing Column 1"), it ends there. Then we kick back over to sklearn, who notices the key error and informs us. This may seem like it's easily solvable (e.g. "Just check your columns first!"), but it ends up being incredibly difficult to debug once you're in a deep pipeline with 2-3 transformation steps, themselves potentially going into nested column transformers and pipelines. Throw in custom classes that also rename your columns, and more than 1-2 misnamed/missing features, and it becomes very time consuming. ### Describe your proposed solution It would be a huge quality of life improvement to list all columns which are causing the key-error, instead of just the first. Normally I address this by just patching my copy of sklearn in . Around line 450 in : Which yields this more informative traceback: Looks like a similar approach might be usable to modify the function a few lines down, but I haven't tested this on it. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant See above manual patching (certainly not the best solution however) ### Additional context I am willing to have a go at making this PR if you'd like!

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Potential NameError in _gpc.py when eval_gradient is False
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact Hi! While exploring the gaussian_process module for GSoC preparation, I noticed a potential NameError in _gpc.py around line 399. If eval_gradient is False, K_gradient is not defined, but the code later attempts to access it in a loop. I have a fix ready and would love to submit a PR if this is confirmed as an issue! ### Steps/Code to Reproduce import numpy as np from sklearn.gaussian_process import GaussianProcessClassifier from sklearn.gaussian_process.kernels import RBF # 1. Setup simple data X = np.array([[0], [1], [2]]) y = np.array([0, 1, 0]) # 2. Fit the model gpc = GaussianProcessClassifier(kernel=RBF()) gpc.fit(X, y) # 3. Trigger the log_marginal_likelihood with eval_gradient=False # This should theoretically fail at line 399 in _gpc.py # because K_gradient is accessed but not defined. theta = gpc.kernel_.theta try: lml = gpc.log_marginal_likelihood(theta, eval_gradient=False) print(f"LML: {lml}") except NameError as e: print(f"Caught expected NameError: {e}") ### Expected Results The function should return the log-marginal-likelihood (Z) without attempting to calculate or access gradients when eval_gradient is set to False. ### Actual Results >A NameError: name 'K_gradient' is not defined occurs when the loop at line 398 attempts to access K_gradient, because the variable was only initialized inside the if eval_gradient: block. ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug Yes, I am interested in fixing this and can provide a PR once the issue is triaged.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: ENH: Add FrequencyEncoder to sklearn.preprocessing
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable When working with high-cardinality categorical columns (e.g. city names, product IDs, user IDs), users need a simple, leak-proof way to encode categories as their frequency in the training set. Currently users must hand-roll this inside a pipeline or install external libraries like category_encoders or feature-engine, which is error-prone and adds unnecessary dependencies. Example: enc = FrequencyEncoder(normalize=True) enc.fit_transform(X[['city']]) # London (50% of data) → 0.50 # Paris (33% of data) → 0.33 # Tokyo (17% of data) → 0.17 ### Describe your proposed solution Add a FrequencyEncoder transformer to sklearn.preprocessing with the following API: FrequencyEncoder( normalize=True, # True = relative freq, False = raw count handle_unknown=0.0, # value for unseen categories at transform time min_frequency=None, # collapse rare labels below this threshold ) Key design decisions: - Works inside Pipeline and ColumnTransformer (no data leakage) - Implements get_feature_names_out() - Supports set_output(transform="pandas") - Mirrors OrdinalEncoder conventions for consistency - Multiple columns encoded independently in one transformer ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context This encoding technique is widely used in tabular ML competitions (Kaggle) and production pipelines. It is simple, target-free, and produces a single compact column regardless of cardinality — making it more scalable than OneHotEncoder for large categorical vocabularies. I am willing to implement this if maintainers agree it belongs in core.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: CI Add aggregating job in GHA to summarize statuses of all unit test jobs and be used a required check
Body: Follow-up on the idea would be to have a single ggregating job (green if all unit test jobs are green, red if one of the unit test is red) to use it as required checks. Azure had a built-in aggregated job which we were using for our required check and right now our GHA required checks are based on a number of exact job names which is a bit brittle. From a quick look some time ago - [github.com/orgs/community/discussions/4324#discussioncomment- ]( - third-party GitHub action [re-actors/alls-green]( that seems to handle this use case I am wondering whether if this is doable/easy to take into account the status "optional" jobs, for example free-threaded or scipy-dev in a PR. ---------------- More details, GitHub required checks are managed centrally via the web UI on a job level and are based on exact job names. Setting is [here]( That means that it you change a job name in and then update the required checks, all the PRs will have a pending job that never complete and the PR can not be merged. The work-around is to merge into your PR branch. Having a single aggregating job used a I think @FrancoisPgm had an initial look at one point, feel free to add more relevant context if you have some.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: docs: document speciesist bias as recognized category in fairness documentation
Body: > [!WARNING] > This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines]( and in particular the sections for [new contributors]( and the ["Needs triage"]( label. ## Proposal Add speciesist bias as a recognized bias category in scikit-learn's fairness and bias documentation. ## Precedent Within scikit-learn scikit-learn already took a significant step by removing the Boston Housing dataset due to racial bias (PR #20729). This demonstrated the project's commitment to addressing documented biases in ML tools and datasets. ## Academic Foundation Speciesist bias in AI/ML is now documented across multiple peer-reviewed publications: - **Hagendorff et al. (2023).** "Speciesist bias in AI." *AI and Ethics*. Found speciesist bias in image recognition, word embeddings, and language models. - **Takeshita et al. (2022).** Found BERT, RoBERTa associate harmful words with nonhuman animals. *Information Processing & Management*. - **Hagendorff et al. (2025).** SpeciesismBench (1,003 items): LLMs "frequently normalized harm toward farmed animals." ## Relevance to scikit-learn - **Text classification**: Models trained with TF-IDF or other text features inherit speciesist associations from training corpora - **Sentiment analysis**: Vocabulary-based sentiment tools rate violence toward animals differently than equivalent violence described with other terms - **Embedding-based features**: Word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) encode speciesist associations that transfer to downstream classifiers - **Dataset documentation**: Datasets involving animal-related text or agricultural data may encode speciesist assumptions ## Suggested Change A brief mention in the fairness/bias documentation sections that speciesist bias is a documented phenomenon in ML, with citations. This helps practitioners consider a broader range of biases when evaluating their models.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: DOC: Clarify max_features default difference between RandomForestClassifier and RandomForestRegressor
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation While building a Random Forest model for a course project, I found myself confused by the different default values for max_features in classifiers vs regressors and had to search externally to understand why. In the ensemble documentation ( the parameter is mentioned with different defaults for classifiers ( ) vs regressors ( ), but there is no explanation of why these defaults differ or how changing this parameter affects model performance in practice. As a student learning to use Random Forests, I found this confusing when building my first models. The docs state that affects the bias variance tradeoff but do not clarify the direction of this effect or provide a concrete example. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix ## Suggested improvement 1. Add a brief explanation of why the defaults differ between classifier and regressor (the sqrt heuristic comes from Breiman's original paper for classification; using all features for regression is standard practice to reduce bias) 2. Clarify that lower → more randomness between trees → lower variance but potentially higher bias 3. Add a short code example showing how affects out-of-bag score or test accuracy so users can see the tradeoff empirically ## References - Breiman, L. (2001). Random Forests. Machine Learning, 45(1), 5–32. - Current docs: [ (

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Suggestion: reference a RAG / LLM debugging checklist (WFGY Problem Map) for downstream pipelines
Body: Hi scikit-learn team, thank you for keeping such an essential ML toolkit alive and healthy. Many modern LLM / RAG stacks still rely on scikit-learn components for preprocessing, feature pipelines, classical models, and evaluation baselines around an LLM core. I maintain an MIT-licensed project called **WFGY Problem Map**, which is a 16-question diagnostic checklist for real-world RAG / LLM pipelines. It focuses on failure modes that happen in the data and retrieval layers (ingestion, chunking, indexing, vector search, evaluation gaps), not on fine-tuning the LLM itself. Why I think this might be relevant for scikit-learn users: - A lot of production RAG systems still combine scikit-learn pipelines with vector stores and LLMs. - Several of the 16 failure modes talk about how features, labels, and evaluation metrics can drift once an LLM layer is added on top. - The checklist is model- and framework-agnostic, so it does not depend on any deep learning library. WFGY Problem Map has already been referenced by a few external ecosystems, for example: - Harvard MIMS Lab’s ToolUniverse - Multimodal RAG Survey by QCRI LLM Lab - Rankify (University of Innsbruck) Concrete suggestion: If you feel it fits, would you consider adding a short “See also” style link in your docs or community resources, for users who are extending scikit-learn workflows into LLM / RAG pipelines? > “RAG / LLM debugging checklist: WFGY Problem Map (16 failure modes)” > Project home: Thank you for considering and for all your work on scikit-learn. Best, PSBigBig

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Wrong chance_level plot in PrecisionRecallDisplay when using Pytorch data
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact The chance_level line drawn by when is not correct when is an instance of . ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results Correct chance classifier line at y 0.4 with y_true as a numpy array. ### Actual Results Wrong chance classifier line at y 0.0 with y_true as a pytorch tensor. ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug Working on this, the root cause is due to these reasons: - pytorch (and potentially other array libraries) defines scalars in such a way that their hash is different from their value and same values may have different hashes; - relies on class to compare entries with values in order to generate (chance level). - is not able to group objects with same values since they have different hash values. Comparing the values from directly to to calculate should be enough to fix this.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: learning_curve with exploit_incremental_learning=True fails to compute training scores when sample_weight is provided
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact When using learning_curve with exploit_incremental_learning=True and passing sample_weight (or other parameters) to the scorer, the training scores for all training sizes except the final one are returned as NaN. This happens because _incremental_fit_estimator pre-computes the training scores' parameters once for the full training set, but then attempts to use them for subsets of the training data. This causes a ValueError (consistent length mismatch) inside the scorer, which is silently caught and replaced with NaN. This affects any user trying to evaluate learning curves incrementally while using weighted metrics or metadata-aware scorers. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results The training scores should be successfully computed for all training sizes. Example: [[0.96 0.96 0.96], [0.94 0.94 0.94], [0.92 0.92 0.92]] (no NaN values). ### Actual Results ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug Yes, I am interested in fixing this.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: 🚀 Feature Request: Integrate (Random k Conditional Nearest Neighbor) into scikit-learn-compatible API
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable ### Summary This issue proposes integrating **Random k Conditional Nearest Neighbor (RkCNN)** — as described in *PeerJ Computer Science (2025)* — into a fully scikit-learn-compliant estimator interface, enabling seamless use with pipelines, cross-validation, and hyperparameter tuning. Paper: Reference implementation (authors): --- ### Motivation Nearest neighbor methods degrade in high-dimensional settings due to irrelevant/noisy features. RkCNN addresses this by: - Sampling multiple random feature subsets - Training kCNN models on each subset - Computing a **separation score** (between-class / within-class variance ratio) - Weighting or filtering subsets based on their discriminative power - Aggregating predictions via weighted probability averaging This approach improves robustness in high-dimensional problems (e.g., gene expression, embeddings, sparse features) while maintaining the simplicity and interpretability of kNN-style classifiers. However, the current implementation: - Does not fully adhere to modern scikit-learn API standards - Lacks strict estimator validation ( ) - Does not clearly separate model logic from sklearn mixins - Could benefit from vectorization and improved maintainability A cleaned, standardized implementation would make RkCNN production-ready and interoperable with: - - - - Model persistence ( ) - scikit-learn-compatible tooling --- ### Proposed Improvements 1. Refactor into a fully compliant estimator: - Inherit from and - Implement , , - Ensure / compatibility 2. Improve internal structure: - Separate subset sampling, scoring, and aggregation logic - Vectorize separation score computation - Avoid redundant neighbor model instantiation where possible 3. Add: - Deterministic - Input validation using , - Unit tests - Documentation and examples 4. Optional enhancements: - Parallel subset training ( ) - Support for sparse input - Option to toggle weighting vs. top-r filtering --- ### Expected Benefits - Stronger high-dimensional classifier within sklearn ecosystem - Drop-in alternative to vanilla kNN - Better robustness without heavy feature engineering - Compatible with modern ML workflows --- ### Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Fully sklearn-compatible estimator - [ ] Passes - [ ] Supports - [ ] Reproducible via - [ ] Unit tests included - [ ] Documentation example added ### Describe your proposed solution Implement within or as appropriate for first class support. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant None. ### Additional context None.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Footer: BNP Paribas sponsor logo is missing (broken image) in footer
Body: On the scikit-learn documentation website footer, the BNP Paribas sponsor logo does not render correctly. Instead of the logo image, only the fallback text "BNP" is displayed, which suggests the image asset is missing or failing to load. Steps to reproduce: 1. Open 2. Scroll to the footer 3. Observe the BNP sponsor logo area Expected behavior: The BNP Paribas logo image should be displayed correctly. Actual behavior: Only the fallback text "BNP" is shown (broken or missing image). Screenshot attached. I am happy to investigate or submit a fix if this is confirmed.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: The eigsh call in spectral embedding is done wrong and differently from what is described in the comments
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact The way is run in the spectral embedding routine is suboptimal and not consistent with what is described in the comments there. Here are the relevant bits from The comments say that is supposed to be run on and the eigenvalues of interest are close to 1, which is why is used. This makes sense. However, in the code, the eigsh is run on . Of course it has the same eigenvectors, but the relevant eigenvalues are close to 0. So setting does not make any sense. To make the code follow what is described in the comments, one would need to add ones to the diagonal of prior to running . The user-facing impact is that the runtime is slower than it could be (see below). ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results Runtime 1.5 sec on my machine and the eiganvalues . ### Actual Results Runtime 10.1 sec on my machine and eigenvalues . ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug One-line fix.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: TimeSeries Split: Allow for larger test_size than n_samples // (n_splits + 1)
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable I have a time series y with sequence length 1800. Each time stamp is equivalent to 2 seconds, meaning the whole time series covers a time span of 3600 seconds or 1 hour. I want to use the first 5 minutes/ 300 seconds/150 time stamps to predict the remaining time series. Therefore, I want to do a 3-fold time-series cross-validation during the training of the forecasting model. This means that in each split, I take the first 50 time stamps to train the model, and the next 1650 time stamps to validate the model. In the third fold, I then use 150 time stamps (5 minutes) and predict the remaining 1650 time stamps in my series. When creating the splitter with When looping through the split with I get the error: which is due to the maximum test_size of . I wonder why it is not possible to have much larger test sets comparred to train sets? ### Describe your proposed solution I could, of course, write my own splitter, but I think it would be a good improvement to have this capability also in the framework to make it more complete. Introducing a gap to only forecast the end of the time series is not an option for me due to the nature of my forecasting algorithm. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Redundant execution of check_class_weight_balanced_linear_classifier
Body: ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact In sklearn/utils/estimator_checks.py, the function _yield_classifier_checks yields check_class_weight_balanced_linear_classifier twice for linear classifiers that support the class_weight parameter. This bug was discovered during a manual code review of estimator_checks.py. It affects any scikit-learn user or developer who uses check_estimator (or the underlying check generators) to validate estimators. The impact is redundant test execution. For example, when running common tests for a LogisticRegression or a custom linear classifier using the mixin, this specific check (which involves fitting the estimator and verifying class weight handling) is executed twice. This is wasteful in terms of CI/CD time and can lead to duplicated error messages or logs, making debugging more tedious. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression from sklearn.utils.estimator_checks import _yield_classifier_checks def get_name(check): return getattr(check, "__name__", getattr(check, "func", check).__name__) clf = LogisticRegression() checks = list(_yield_classifier_checks(clf)) check_names = [get_name(c) for c in checks] duplicate_count = check_names.count( 'check_class_weight_balanced_linear_classifier' ) print(f"Yield count: {duplicate_count}") ### Expected Results The check should be yielded exactly once. Yield count: 1 ### Actual Results The check is currently yielded twice. Yield count: 2 ### Versions ### Interest in fixing the bug I am interested in fixing this bug. I have already identified the root cause and implemented a fix. Analysis of root cause: In _yield_classifier_checks, there are two consecutive if blocks that both check for isinstance(classifier, LinearClassifierMixin) and the presence of class_weight in parameters. Both blocks yield the same check.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Add built-in utilities for high-dimensional biological data preprocessing (gene expression, omics data)
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable Many users apply scikit-learn to biological datasets such as gene expression matrices, RNA-seq counts, proteomics, and metabolomics data. These datasets usually have: - Very high dimensionality (thousands of genes/features) - Strong sparsity or zero inflation - Batch effects and technical noise - Requirement for specific normalization and filtering steps Currently, users must rely on external libraries (scanpy, bioconductor, custom scripts) before they can use scikit-learn models. I want to enable a workflow where: - Raw biological feature matrices (e.g., genes × samples) can be directly processed. - Common bioinformatics preprocessing steps are available as sklearn-style transformers. - These transformers can be used inside Pipeline and GridSearchCV. This would make scikit-learn more accessible for bioinformatics and omics researchers. ### Describe your proposed solution Add a small submodule or set of transformers focused on high-dimensional biological - data, for example: **GeneFilter** - Remove genes with low variance, low counts, or low presence across samples. **LogNormalize** - Apply log(1 + x) or similar transformations common in RNA-seq and microarray analysis. **BatchEffectScaler** - Simple batch-aware scaling using provided batch labels. **ZeroInflationFilter** - Remove features dominated by zeros. All transformers should: - Follow sklearn API: fit, transform, fit_transform - Work with NumPy arrays and pandas DataFrames - Integrate with Pipeline ### Example usage: ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant Currently, users rely on: - Scanpy / AnnData - Bioconductor (R ecosystem) - Custom preprocessing scripts However, these tools are not tightly integrated with scikit-learn Pipelines and cross-validation tools. Bridging this gap would reduce friction between bioinformatics preprocessing and machine learning modeling. ### Additional context Bioinformatics, genomics, and omics-based machine learning is a fast-growing field. Adding even basic biological-data-aware preprocessing tools would: - Attract more researchers from life sciences - Reduce dependency on external preprocessing stacks - Make scikit-learn more friendly for interdisciplinary research This proposal does not aim to replace specialized bioinformatics libraries, but to provide minimal, general-purpose utilities that cover common preprocessing needs before machine learning.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: BUG: LinearRegression is wrong on float32 and large n_samples (> 500k rows) due to param to scipy.linalg.lstsq
Body: ### Describe the bug is wrong on with a large sample size (e.g. 500k rows) since sklearn 1.6.0. This is still the case on 1.8.0. See minimal repro below. The residual sum of squares is much larger than the solution found by on . Using the minimal repro below, points to this commit from - Indeed, changing L702 to fixes the bug. - Doing so will revive issue #26164, so someone needs to make a call. - I think issue 26164 is strictly less important than the correctness of linear regression (minimize residue). - at L702 scales up linearly with the number of rows and eventually zeros out large enough singular values to throw off the solution completely. - As a result, sklearn fails to scale to even 500k rows at precision below. - Meanwhile, (the underlying solver) and don't have this issue with the internally defaulted / values (see inline comments below). ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results ### Actual Results ### Root cause analysis Why does sklearn set this way in the first place? [#30040]( explains: > The solver can fail the sample weight consistency test, especially for wide dataset ( ) after centering (as done when ). > > Setting the parameter (cut-off ratio on singular values) to the value recommended by documentation seems to fix the bug. - Indeed, defaults to [ ]( - However, for float32 input, numpy sets (calculation precision) to float64 and (result precision) to float32 ([two lines above]( so numpy does not have the bug described by the current issue. - Calc type is always float64: [here]( and [there ( )]( - This comes at a cost. float32 X will be cast into a float64 copy by numpy's [ufunc mechanism]( - > Casting will be used as necessary. The actual DType of the input arrays is not considered unless signature is None for that array. - sklearn's underlying solver is . For float32 input, it sets calculation precision to (no casting / copy), unlike numpy's . - This does not interact well with default , resulting in this bug. - The above can be validated by pdb-ing into the corresponding lines in the minimal repro above. ### Suggested fix First add the above minimal repro as a new test case. Then add to #30040's test case with this patch: Click to expand patch! Then do TDD to find a combination of (dtype, cond) to pass all tests. I haven't been able to find anything reasonable. I think it is more urgent to fix to meet its definition (minimize residue sum of squares) than to pass the [sample weight consistency tests]( In the worst case I recommend setting , marking the weight tests as xfail, reopening #26164, and maybe report #26164 upstream (to scipy). ### Versions

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Add Net Benefit for classifications metrics
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable Net benefit is defined as a decision-analytic measure that quantifies the clinical value of a prediction model, test, or marker by putting its benefits and harms on the same scale. This is done by specifying an exchange rate—a clinical judgment about how much a harm (e.g., an unnecessary biopsy) can be tolerated in order to gain a benefit (e.g., detecting a cancer). The net benefit calculation then subtracts weighted harms from benefits to determine whether using the model or test would do more good than harm in practice, unlike traditional statistics such as sensitivity or AUC that do not directly reflect clinical value. ### Describe your proposed solution I would like to implement this into , but want to make sure there's no major objection for adding new metrics (and later add a decision analysis curve as well: ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_

Proposed fix: store at the wrapper level like does, and adjust validation code paths. Repro: Observed failure (branch: ): ## Researcher Specification - cross_val_predict handling for enforces class order via , expecting a top-level that is indexable per output when predictions is a list. - In this branch, does: which requires at the wrapper level. - (in ) currently does not define at the wrapper level after . Each underlying estimator has its own . - in the same module sets during . ### Proposed Fix - Modify to set a wrapper-level after (and update on if applicable), mirroring : - In , within : - Add to Attributes docstring: - — list of length ; each element is an array of class labels for the corresponding output. - Implement: - Optionally: - No changes needed in validation: it already handles list predictions and expects . ### Tests - Add regression tests (e.g., in ): 1) LDA + with should return a list of length ; each array shape with rows summing to 1. 2) Mixed binary/multiclass outputs (e.g., base) maintain correct shapes and order. 3) without remains working for multi-output classifiers. ### Risks/Notes - If any sub-estimator lacks , raises ; will propagate accordingly. - Class order differences across folds are handled by (existing behavior). - Multi-output with different class sets per target is represented naturally by list-of-arrays . We will implement this fix on a dedicated branch and open a single PR targeting .

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: clone: treat class-valued parameters as non-estimators (avoid TypeError) [swev-id: scikit-learn__scikit-learn-12585]
Body: ## User request Clone fails for parameters that are estimator types. Key: clone should not error when a parameter is a class; adjust clone to treat classes as non-estimators (e.g., check isinstance(estimator, type) or ismethod(get_params)). ## Research specification - Location: sklearn/base.py, function . - Problem: When an estimator has a parameter whose value is a class/type (e.g., rather than an instance), attempts to call on the class object, leading to . - Proposed change: Add an early guard in after container-type handling: - - Treat Python classes/types as non-estimators and leave them unchanged, both at top level and within parameter traversal. - Edge cases: - Estimator parameter set to a scikit-learn estimator class → preserved unchanged. - Parameter set to a user-defined class type (not ) → preserved unchanged. - numpy scalar types (e.g., ) → preserved unchanged as types. - typing/dataclass types → preserved unchanged. - Tests: - New tests to ensure cloning succeeds and preserves identity for class-valued parameters; estimator instances continue to be cloned. - Risk & API consistency: - Minimal, backward-compatible change; relaxes previous erroneous behavior when encountering class objects. Normal estimator instance cloning behavior is unchanged.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: GridSearchCV.verbose documentation does not match actual behavior (values >3, >10)
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation [ docs ]( state > **verbose** int > Controls the verbosity: the higher, the more messages. > \>1 : the computation time for each fold and parameter candidate is displayed; > \>2 : the score is also displayed; > \>3 : the fold and candidate parameter indexes are also displayed together with the starting time of the computation. In reality, this param is passed to joblib [here]( whose [docs]( state: > **verbose**: int, default=0 > The verbosity level: if non zero, progress messages are printed. Above 50, the output is sent to stdout. The frequency of the messages increases with the verbosity level. If it more than 10, all iterations are reported. Testing with the MRE below shows that 0 is silent, 1 prints one line, 2 prints a line for each CV split. 3-9 are all the same. 10-inf are all the same ### MRE: It is possible taht the same issue exists elsewhere, I've not looked thoroughly ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix Update docs to match observed behaviour, e.g.: > **verbose** int > Controls the verbosity: the higher, the more messages. > 0 : no messages; > 1 : a single line describing the number of folds, the number of candidates, and the number of fits; > 2 : the above, plus when each fold ends, the computation time for that fold and parameter candidate is displayed; > 3-9 : the above, plus the fold index and the score is also displayed; > ≥10 : an additional message for the start of each fold, and the candidate parameter indexes are also displayed.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Add Apriori Algorithm for Association Rule Mining
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable I would like scikit-learn to support association rule mining by implementing the Apriori algorithm. This would allow users to efficiently find frequent itemsets and generate association rules directly within the scikit-learn framework, similar to other supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms. Use cases include market basket analysis, recommendation systems, and pattern discovery in categorical datasets. The workflow would involve: - Inputting transactional or categorical data. - Generating frequent itemsets with a minimum support threshold. - Producing association rules with metrics such as confidence, lift, and leverage. ### Describe your proposed solution Implement an Apriori class or function in the sklearn.preprocessing or sklearn.association module that provides: - fit() to generate frequent itemsets. - generate_rules() to produce association rules. - Support for configurable metrics: support, confidence, lift, leverage. - Compatibility with pandas DataFrames and NumPy arrays. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant Currently, Python libraries like mlxtend provide Apriori and association rule mining. However, integrating it into scikit-learn would: - Ensure consistent API with scikit-learn estimators. - Enable seamless integration with pipelines and transformers. - Benefit users who prefer a single framework for all ML tasks. ### Additional context - Association rule mining is a common unsupervised learning task, widely used in industry. - Many users are already using mlxtend alongside scikit-learn, but a native implementation would improve performance, maintainability, and ecosystem consistency.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Memory Leak in Logistic Regression.
Body: ### Describe the bug Extreme RAM spike and OOM after upgrade (0.24.2 → 1.1.1) for ultra‑wide sparse L1 Logistic Regression (liblinear & saga) – baseline 680 GB → >1.4 TB (ref #28993) Summary After upgrading scikit‑learn and Python, fitting an L1 Logistic Regression on a very large, sparse, high‑dimensional dataset now triggers a massive memory spike (baseline ~680 GB → allocates >1.4 TB → OOM). Previously (with 0.24.2) peak memory stabilized around 750–770 GB for the same data shape. Switching solvers (liblinear → saga) and using SGDClassifier did not resolve the issue. Workload Scale Samples: ~7,500,000 Features: ~550,000 Binary classification, L1 regularization Input matrix: sparse (CSR) Environments Stable (old): Python 3.9 scikit-learn 0.24.2 numpy 1.23.5 scipy 1.7.1 Peak during fit: ~750–770 GB Problematic (new): Python 3.10 scikit-learn 1.1.1 scipy 1.14.1 numpy 1.23.5 Baseline resident memory before calling .fit: ~680 GB (holding data & process) During .fit: climbs rapidly past ~1.4 TB → OOM Models Tried Original: Python LogisticRegression( solver='liblinear', C=1.0, penalty='l1', intercept_scaling=3.0, random_state=123, ) Alternative: Python SGDClassifier( loss='log_loss', penalty='l1', alpha=1.0, random_state=123, ) Attempt with saga (still spikes): Python LogisticRegression( solver='saga', C=1.0, penalty='l1', max_iter=1000, n_jobs=-1, random_state=123, ) New Observation Memory right before training (after data load / preprocessing) sits around 680 GB. Training triggers a single large jump rather than gradual leakage across multiple fits. This suggests a large dense working allocation or multiple parallel buffers rather than slow accumulation. Questions Are there known regressions or algorithmic changes between 0.24.2 and 1.1.1 that increase memory for sparse, high‑feature L1 logistic regression (liblinear or saga)? Does liblinear (L1) or saga under certain conditions implicitly densify parts of X at this scale? Could n_jobs=-1 for saga (or OpenMP threads) multiply large temporary buffers? Is single-thread execution recommended for memory control here? Is intercept_scaling=3.0 relevant for any hidden copy or augmentation step that becomes costly at extreme dimensionality? Are there scipy 1.14.1 sparse changes that alter memory layout or workspace usage compared to what 0.24.2 relied on? Recommended solver / pipeline for this regime (7.5M × 550k, very sparse, L1): saga vs SGD vs feature hashing + SGD vs dimensionality reduction first? Would upgrading to a newer scikit-learn (1.5.x / 1.6.dev) likely help (any memory PRs touching saga/liblinear)? ### Steps/Code to Reproduce LogisticRegression( solver='liblinear', C=1.0, penalty='l1', intercept_scaling=3.0, random_state=123, ) ### Expected Results The peak memory usage should be below 780Gb. ### Actual Results The memory is exceeding 1.5Tb ### Versions

Title: Clarify docs for allowable KDTree metrics
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation currently states, for the metrics kwarg: "Metric to use for distance computation. Default is “minkowski”, which results in the standard Euclidean distance when p = 2. A list of valid metrics for KDTree is given by the attribute valid_metrics. See the documentation of [scipy.spatial.distance]( and the metrics listed in [distance_metrics]( for more information on any distance metric." Yet clicking on yields the following list of metrics: cityblock, cosine, euclidean, haversine, l1, l2, manhattan, nan_euclidean, which is different from the actual list of supported metrics (euclidean, l2, minkowski, p, manhattan, cityblock, l1, chebyshev, infinity). I could not find this list other than by actually printing out the value of . ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix Explicitly list the actually supported metrics in the docs (or at least, not link to a list that is different). A few minor related possible improvements: - There's a formatting issue immediately after the kwarg in likely the "Note:" should be moved to a real rst note (or indented as part of the doc for the metric kwarg). - The list in could group together equivalent metrics ("'l1', 'cityblock', 'manhattan': metrics.pairwise.manhattan_distance; 'l2', 'euclidean': ..., )

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Reduce “Can I take this?” by front-loading /take rule
Body: ## Summary New contributors frequently comment “Can I take this?” on issues without the *help wanted* label. This creates notification noise and does not trigger CI assignment. The current contributing docs lead with “apply the ‘help wanted’ label to find issues… then comment ”, which is easy to mislead as “ is only for ‘help wanted’ issues”. ## evidence - **Maintainers have posted warnings like “comments such as ‘can I work on this’ or ‘assign this to me’ may get you banned” on issues already being worked on.** - **Recent examples of “Can I take this?” / “assign this to me” appear across issues.** (*The following images are only used to demonstrate the existence of this situation and have no other meaning.*) - **This noise hides actionable updates and shifts cognitive load to maintainers.** ## cause - The contribution guidelines are lengthy and detailed, which may make it difficult for beginners to quickly grasp the key points. If the opening example emphasizes a certain label, it can easily be misunderstood as "this is the only situation that can be done".In Contributing Doc,it emphasizes “help wanted” as the easy way to find issues and then says to comment . For some new contributer and skimmers, the mental model becomes: ***help wanted → allowed to /take; everything else → ask first***. - Many newcomers, out of politeness or lack of confidence, will first ask, "Can I do this?" to avoid doing something pointless or infringing on others' territory. This reflects their uncertainty about community norms. - The doc later explains time windows, stale/claimed PRs, etc., but many newcomers never read that. - Placing “filter by *help wanted* … then comment ” early in the doc can plausibly mislead newcomers due to well-documented effects: [**Serial-position effect**]( makes early information disproportionately influential, so readers treat it as the operative rule;and the [**Web User Behaviour Directed by Information Scent**]( and the [**Default Effect**]( shows people often follow an implied “recommended” option when uncertain. Taken together, these explain why newcomers may ask “Can I take this?” on non-labelled issues. ## Proposal 1. **Front-load the universal rule right in the note box:** - “ works for any unclaimed issue; the ‘help wanted’ label is only a discovery aid.” 2. **Add a one-sentence callout to discourage noise:** - “Please avoid comments like ‘Can I take this?’—use ' instead. If you need design feedback, briefly outline your plan and then .” 3. **Link “how to check if an issue is already taken / time windows” from that same box.** ## Why do this? - This does not change policy; it clarifies and surfaces the existing policy. - No restructuring of the entire page—just a small copy edit and one cross-link. - Reduce the noise.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: ENH: Add overlay_dx_score metric for regression evaluation
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable ## Current Problem When evaluating regression models, especially for time series forecasting, existing metrics (MAE, RMSE, R²) don't capture how well predictions align visually with actual values across different tolerance levels. ## Desired Workflow ## Use Cases 1. Time series forecasting evaluation where visual alignment matters 2. Scenarios where outlier sensitivity of MSE/RMSE is problematic 3. Understanding prediction quality at multiple tolerance levels 4. Model selection when alignment is more important than absolute error ## Proposed Implementation Add function to . ### Function Signature ### How It Works 1. Define tolerance intervals as percentages of the data range 2. For each tolerance level, compute percentage of predictions within tolerance 3. Calculate area under the resulting curve 4. Normalize by maximum possible area ### Mathematical Formula where: - A = area under the overlay percentage curve - A_max = maximum possible area (max_percentage × 100) ### Key Features - Supports (sklearn standard) - Supports regression - Less sensitive to outliers than MSE/RMSE - Provides interpretable score (0 to 1 range) ### Implementation Details - Pure NumPy implementation, no external dependencies - Vectorized operations for efficiency - Follows sklearn API conventions - Complete numpydoc documentation - 95%+ test coverage ready ## References - Repository: - Currently used internally for time series forecasting evaluation - Compatible implementation exists: works with cross_val_score, GridSearchCV, Pipeline ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_
