instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Inconsistent Results For Logistic Regressions across multiple computers
Body: ### Describe the bug Hey all - I'm working on teaching some students some logistic regressions and noticed different computers can produce slightly different intercepts/coefs. At first I thought it was maybe environment differences, but I have been able to reproduce the variances when accounting for the various packages/interpreters. I can create a new environment on computer 1 (windows 10, intel 8th gen CPU), computer 2 (windows 10, intel 11th gen CPU), and a coworker's M1 MacBook - all three produce different results. I thought maybe minute differences in Numpy's Openblas or MKL could be the culprit but that yielded the same varying results. I've tried not splitting the data, different random states, different C values, different solvers... For the purposes of reproducibility, I ran this is exact code & uploaded the data to Google sheets: All versions of all other packages are held constant. I received this results across the three computers: | values | Computer 1 with Sklearn 1.1.2 | Computer 2 with Sklearn 1.1.2 | M1 Macbook with Sklearn 1.1.1 | |---|---|---|---| |Intercept| -12. | -12. | -12. | |Age coef| -0.037738| -0.032577 |-0.037685| |Experience coef| 0.039255| 0.034171|0.039202| |Income coef| 0.058843| 0.058837 |0.058844| |Family coef| 0.612243| 0.613192 |0.612251| I have tried many other things that all produce slightly different results that are not captured in the above table. Multicollinearity is present within the data, but I would still expect consistent results. I understand the results are effectively the same and this produces little real world impacts. However, when a student is comparing results and they see 12.4 vs 12.6 which is a decently large difference. I think some of these differences are too large to just be contributed to floating-point computations -- especially for size of the data. I assume I'm missing something, but I'm reaching the end of my rope on troubleshooting. Thoughts / expected behavior? ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results | values | Computer 1 with Sklearn 1.1.2 | Computer 2 with Sklearn 1.1.2 | M1 Macbook with Sklearn 1.1.1 | |---|---|---|---| |Intercept| -12. | -12. | -12. | |Age coef| -0.037738| -0.032577 |-0.037685| |Experience coef| 0.039255| 0.034171|0.039202| |Income coef| 0.058843| 0.058837 |0.058844| |Family coef| 0.612243| 0.613192 |0.612251| ### Actual Results n/a ### Versions

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: A note about documentation on the logistic regression.
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation Dear Authors. First of all, let me express my respect for your hard work and success, acclaimed and used by thousands of ML specialists. I would like to report a small issue in the documentation, providing a justification for my concern and proposal. The documentation [says]( > Logistic regression, despite its name, is a linear model for classification rather than regression. which is not true, alas. Logistic regression, invented and further developed in terms of the **Generalized Linear Model** and **Generalized Additive Model** by Cox, Nelder, Hastie, Tibshirani and others, served exactly to address regression problems with categorical outcomes (via Maximum Likelihood Estimation). Every single regression model predicts some **statistic** (typically expected value, but can be also quantile, trimmed mean) **of the conditional distribution of the response**, which is always **a numerical outcome**. This applies to the linear, logistic, probit, ordinal, multinomial, Poisson (BTW, this explains why it returns fractions rather than integers), negative binomial, fractional logistic regression, beta, gamma, Cox, and many, many more regression models. The logistic regression is no different - the conditional distribution here is Bernoulli (or binomial with k=1), and the interpretation of the E(Y|X=x) is by def. the probability of the event (if success/occurrence/presence is coded as 1 and the failure/absence as 0). In terms of the Generalized Linear Model, it's simply a GLM with binomial/Bernoulli conditional distribution and logit link. Or simpler - binomial regression with logit link. There are lots of monographs devoted exclusively to the regression aspects of the LR. There are also materials written by typically ML-oriented specialists like Prof. Andrew Ng (Stanford), placing it in frames of the GLM in his papers ([example]( page 22) and admitting this can be used for both applications: regression and classification. I will put also a few book covers below for reference. For the last 60+ years the logistic regression has been one of the **core regression tools in experimental science** to analyse trials/studies with binary endpoints in: clinical trials (drug evaluation and approval part of the pharmaceutical industry), clinical laboratory diagnostics, epidemiology, ecology, econometric, medical biostatistics, actuary, experimental physics, psychology, toxicology and so on, including the causal analysis in these areas. This is also my daily regression tool to make inference in drug approval studies. Please check this query: [ OK, but how is it used for regression purposes? It assesses how a certain set of independent variables affects the odds-ratio or % of successes (depending on formulation; doesn't matter) through the assessment of the: - marginal and interaction effects: direction, magnitude, inference (p-value, confidence interval) - simple effects (for categorical covariates): magnitude of difference/ratio + inference (p-value, CI) ... and, when the model is followed by the AN(C)OVA-like type analysis, here called the "_analysis of deviance"_, it gives us also the main and their interaction effects, e.g. "does gender and treatment affect the % of successes". It's done either via Likelihood Ratio tests of Wald's joint analysis of the model coefficients. The analysis [employs the EM-means ]( (estimated marginal means), which - again - is a typical regression outcome. It is used also in the [IPW - inverse probability weighting.]( The logistic regression was greatly extended to handle repeated or clustered observations via **GEE** (Generalized Estimated Equations) estimation and **Generalized Linear Mixed Models**. There is also the [Alternating Logistic Regression]( also used to handle repetitions. Further generalizations occur as well, for example the Ordinal Logistic Regression (aka proportional odds regression model) is the [generalization ]( of the Mann-Whitney (Wilcoxon) test with covariates. Further extension includes GAM (to relax linearity in parameters) and regularization (LASSO, ridge) to address multi-collinearity. For multiple classes, that are somehow dependent/ordered/nested we have the [Nested LR]( It shows well, how rich is the set of regression-oriented tools built around the Logistic Regression. As every regression, it is also used for predictions. And now, having the predicted probability, by applying a threshold to it (usually, but not necessarily 50%) one can use it for classification. It's then the **logistic classifier based on the result of the logistic regression**. **There is no conflict of meanings**. Regression is for numerical outcome - and every regression gives numerical outcome by its very definition, including the Logistic Regression (probability is numeric). Classification - for binary outcome - and this is given by the Logistic Classifier. Good! **There is a conflict**, however, if some says that "_logistic regression is not a regression_", because it: 1. denies the basic knowledge about how regression works (conditional expectation), 2. ignores the fact, the the LR is used by big thousands of researchers and data scientists for regression purposes, 3. limits those, who won't be able to complete a basic regression analysis of percentages believing that they have no tool for it. 4. Not to mention, that some learners, who read that, may repeat it during an exam or interview and fail it. The books I mentioned above: ![obraz]( Especially this one explains the idea of regression as the conditional expectation (including the GLM): ![obraz]( ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix To conclude, I would like to propose changing the sentence to: > Logistic regression is a special case of the generalized linear regression model (GLM). In Machine Learning it is used mainly for classification by taking the predicted probability and applying a decision rule to it. This makes the Logistic Classifier. (prof. Ng can be referred) Like on this quick and dirty illustration: ![obraz]( This is a **win-win** definition: 1. agrees with statistical definition of regression 2. agrees with ML terminology 5. finally one doesn't have to deny the logic, common sense and struggle with a question: "why is it named a regression if it classifies stuff?" 6. it teaches people, that there exists the GLM (with extensions), which opens big possibilities in data analysis, if - one day - a ML specialist will have to analyse such data.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: MiniBatchDictionaryLearning confusion over U,V
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation The optimization problem in the documentation is described as $$ \arg\min \frac12 \Vert X-UV\Vert_F^2+\alpha \Vert U\Vert_1 $$ such that $\Vert V_k\Vert_2\leq 1$ for all $k$. This is very confusing, because generally $U$ is the dictionary matrix, and $V$ is the representation matrix (e.g. see the paper linked in the documentation So it should read $$ \arg\min \frac12 \Vert X-UV\Vert_F^2+\alpha \Vert V\Vert_1 $$ such that $\Vert U_k\Vert_2\leq 1$ for all $k$; here $U_k$ denotes the columns of $U$. In other words, the $L_1$ regularization should be performed on the representation matrix $V$, and the $L_2$ norm constraints on the columns (basis vectors) of the matrix $U$. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix It should read $$ \arg\min \frac12 \Vert X-UV\Vert_F^2+\alpha \Vert V\Vert_1 $$ such that $\Vert U_k\Vert_2\leq 1$ for all $k$; here $U_k$ denotes the columns of $U$.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Erroneous use of chi2 in the documentation
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation The [documentation page]( for univariate feature selection suggests that the [ ]( function could be used to rank (and possibly select) features. The problem is that the example given in the documentation is the [Iris data set]( where the features are continuous. The function is however supposed to work with contingency matrices of discrete features (i.e., typically counts, frequencies, or booleans). The use of is therefore likely to be misleading in this context. See [this discussion]( on StackExchange for further details. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix Either find a different use of that is more aligned with the fact that it works with contingency matrices or explain more clearly how it could be used with continuous features.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Segmentation error when calling .fit()
Body: ### Describe the bug Hey all, I'm currently busy working on a solution for a classification problem using LogisticRegression from sklearn.linear_model. I'm training multiple classifiers at the same time with the same hyperparameters and only slightly different input. The largest difference is coming from the number of unique labels. I don't have any issues fitting data on a model with size 1.500.000 x 20000 with ~200 unique labels. I do however run into issues when my training data has size 1.000.000 X 11000 with ~2500 labels. I'm executing these on docker containers but after calling .fit my containers exit and get an exit code of 139 indicating a segmentation error. I'm using the saga solver. As far as I know this is not a python error but underlying cython problem and my hunch is that because of my dimensions I'm getting a stack overflow somewhere. I've already checked out the source code but did not find any conclusive answers. My question is: does anyone have an idea where this actually goes wrong and if there is a workaround? I'm running scikit-learn==1.0.2 with Python 3.7.11 ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results No error is thrown. ### Actual Results Nothing in the traceback. My container exits with status code 139. When running this on Jupyter my kernel exits. ### Versions

Title: Should we continue to support compiler=intelem?
Body: I have an build refactor removing and and only uses that successfully builds our wheels and passes tests. I think it is best to move to a pure implementation first, because there are still some lingering issues . For example, no editable wheels with : does not make it easy to support custom compilers: It is doable, but it requires more complexity. (I have not got the intelem compiler fully working on our CI yet, but it is working locally in Docker). Furthermore, I think our [Intel ICC job]( is not checking the Intel build correctly. Specifically, the following compiles the code correctly with : But a few lines later, we run: which overrides the icc compiled extensions with gcc compiled extensions. One can see that gcc is used in the [build logs]( Also in the logs we see that itself is going to be deprecated: Should we support continue to support and build with the Intel compiler? CC @jeremiedbb @ogrisel

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Support usage of and in cross validation
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable We can currently pass and to s, predictors, etc., at predict time when performing "manual" calls. When performing cross validation, however, there is no way to pass down any params to to use when calling / . I believe this to be a limitation of the library and think all cross-validation related code should allow passing down of said params to the estimator performing the prediction. ### Describe your proposed solution Given scoring is at the core of all cross validation methods within scikit-learn, I believe it will suffice to update the scorer parent classes to accept a new arg (maybe just given prediction probabilities is conceptually a subset of predicting in general, but I'd be open to better naming here if people have a preference, or even updating to ) and then work backwards from there. Doing so, I can see that we'd need to update (new level of nesting indicates methods/functions that call the parent they're nested under): - and - - - nothing needed from here as used in and doesn't have or - - - generic function to be used on estimator --> needs updating. not used anywhere - - - created inside of --> add to fit signature - - generic function to be used on estiamtor --> needs updating. - - generic function to be used on estiamtor --> needs updating. - - no to be used - - already updated from above - - generic function to be used on estiamtor --> needs updating. not used anywhere As for how the arg will be added to method/function signatures, I think in general it depends on what the method/function is doing, e.g. use in situations where only predicting is occuring, but use in situations where other logic is also performed (e.g. fitting, scoring, etc). In the all the above methods/functions, I believe they all perform fitting and predicting (either directly or within a sub-call) so I think it'd just be everywhere. This seems similar to how is currently passed around. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant None ### Additional context Related to [this comment]( I'm happy to take on this work if maintainers are happy for it to be done (I've actually got a branch locally with the relevant code updated, but am yet to update/write new tests in case the proposal is rejected)

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: DOC See Also descriptions do not match for multiple functions/classes
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation While working on a docstring-related pull request (#24259) I noticed that, sometimes, the See Also description for the same function/class does not match. For instance, the description was different depending on the class I looked at: I decided to [investigate it further and see if it was occuring elsewhere (click here to check the gist where I regexed the raw files)]( Today, at commit [ ]( we have 56 functions/classes that have some sort of different descriptions at some See Also. From looking at them at first glance I have sorted the differences I get into 4 categorys: 1. Those where the description is related to the current class/function; - For instance, inside the class, is described as the "equivalent" version. That is a different description from the one we see inside , but inside the context of the class, it makes sense. 2. Those related to extra "\n" on the text; 3. Those that are different per se; - For instance, the descriptions we mentioned earlier have no obvious reason to be different. 4. Those that are related to the docstring pattern. - For instance, we are missing a space after the name of the class/function being referenced. So my script puts it inside the previous description (_extra question: shouldn't it be failing on the numpy doc validation? I was excepting it to be an open docstring from #21350, but this function was already done by #24351_). ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix I was planning on taking a closer look at [the output table I get with the differences in the descriptions]( and coming up with the list of functions/classes that we should change to make it uniform across different occurrences of See Also. Then, as this is a fairly beginner friendly issue, I would edit the issue description and give it a step-by-step (copying most part of what @thomasjpfan did in [#21350]( and let folks looking for a "good first issue" help. I would be happy to make sure the task bullets are up to date. What do the maintainers think? Does it make sense? :) _PS: Of course, we will be forever vulnerable to this kind of issue if we don't create a test (maybe adapted from my [gist]( The problem here is that sometimes it just makes sense to have different descriptions... We can create an "ignore list" maybe, but we can discuss this later._

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Implement p-value splitting criterion for Decision Trees
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable The current list of valid criterions for Decision Trees are: {“squared_error”, “friedman_mse”, “absolute_error”, “poisson”} With regard to regression problems, I have run into numerous situations where I would very much like to make splits on the data based on a t-test statistics comparing the means between the two split subsamples. The primary issue with using MSE or Friedman MSE is that the quality of a split is dependent on both the marginal impact of splitting on a particularly variable, as well as the frequency in which that split is triggered. It's not uncommon where I want to identify splits that have highly different means, even though that splitting rule would come into effect rarely. See this post (I am not the author) for more information on why splitting on p-values could be valuable: ### Describe your proposed solution The solution would be to add a new splitting criterion called "p-value" or "t-test" or something similar. The quality of a split would be measured based on either the p-value or the magnitude of the t-statistic. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_

Title: Different dataset for different models when using StackingClassifier
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable Since stacking is a combination of different models, the different models might have different features i.e is is rather often that the same dataset does not fit different models (or it is a sub-optimal dataset for one of the models). E.g a "LightGBM" classifier can handle categorical values by it self, where we in LogisticRegression have to handle that in the dataset, which then (might) lead to two different datasets, and we can't just parse one dataset to for the training. Or, an SVM would have it's data normalized, where a Decision Tree doesn't care, We can use the to sort of work around this but being able to parse multiple datasets could be such a great option ### Describe your proposed solution Something like ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Example performs data preprocessing before train-test split occurs
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation [This example]( uses the following code X = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X) X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split( X, y, test_size=0.4, random_state=42 ) As can be seen, the data preprocessing occurs before the data is split into a train and test set. It might be insignificant in this particular case. Still, inexperienced people will likely be inclined to copy this example and make the same mistake on a larger scale, potentially resulting in harmful real-world consequences. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix Consequently, it might be advisable to replace this code with the following X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split( X, y, test_size=0.4, random_state=42 ) SS = StandardScaler() X_train = SS.fit_transform(X_train) X_test = SS.transform(X_test)

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: DOC: new example on feature engineering for time-series forecasting with prediction intervals
Body: For EuroScipy 2022, I gave a tutorial on how to use pandas-engineered lagged and windowed features for time series forecasting with scikit-learn regressors. Here is the notebook: - I think it might be worth investing some effort to reuse some of that material to turn it into a tutorialish example for the gallery (and cross-link it with the time-related feature example on the same dataset). Note that this example probably covers too many thinks and we should probably focus on a trim down version. For instance, by removing the experiment with MAPIE that I don't find particularly conclusive at the moment (would need to speed more time to find how to make MAPIE output heteroscedastic prediction intervals on this data in particular). I find that the discussion on sktime to be informative to go beyond the pure-scikit-learn approach which I found out to be limitting in retrospect as explained towards the end of the tutorial. /cc @lorentzenchr who expressed interest. Also /cc @ArturoAmorQ who might be interested in working on such a contribution.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: BUG: MLLE implementation does not yield expected results
Body: ### Describe the bug The implemented manifold learning method Modified Locally Linear Embedding (MLLE) in /sklearn/manifold/_locally_linear.py line 403 ('method==modified') does not produce the expected results, see attached img. From the reference [3] it is clear that MLLE should reconstruct a lower dimesional manifold which preserves local geometric characteristics of the higher-dimensional input data. This means data points of the same color which are close in the 'Original S-curve' 3D data should also be close in reconstructed 2D manifold. Also points which are far away from each other along the local coordinate along the S-shape should be far away in the reconstruction. Meaning the yellow points and the blue points should be the furthest apart in the reconstructed data. As can be seen from the [example]( the current implementation of MLLE does not statisfy these properties. [3] Zhang, Z. & Wang, J. MLLE: Modified Locally Linear Embedding Using Multiple Weights. ( ![MLLE_Bugreport]( ### Steps/Code to Reproduce To reproduce, run the corresponding part of the example [Comparison of Manifold Learning methods]( ### Expected Results The expected result is a manifold which preserves local geometric features, similar to other methods such as 'Isomap', 'LTSA' or 'Hessian Eigenmap' The results shown here are generated with my own implementation of the paper by Zhang, Z. & Wang, J. 'MLLE: Modified Locally Linear Embedding Using Multiple Weights'. However, the notation is from their later publication [5], where they call the method 'NEML', but it is the same algorithm. To implement replace the code of the current MLLE implementation with the following snippet: [5] Wang J., Z. & Zhang, Z. Nonlinear embedding preserving muliple local-linearities. Pattern Recognition. 43 (2010) ![MLLE_ExpectedResult]( ### Actual Results The acutal result is a construction which does not seem to preserve the desired local gemeometric characteristics. ![MLLE_Result]( ### Versions

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: BUG: GaussianProcessRegressor.predict inplace modifies input X, when passed via kernel
Body: ### Describe the bug In line 425, 426 of /sklearn/gaussian_process/_gpr.py (inside the predict method) y_var is modified in place: If a kernel (e.g. a custom kernel) is used that returns X when diag(X) is called (X[:,0] == var == diag(X)) this leads to the modification of the original X vector. So now X == y_var - np.einsum("ij,ji->i", V.T, V). A simple but dirty fix for me is to always make and return a copy, when building a custom kernel: var = np.copy(X[:,0]). But because this error is hard to find and debug, and the documentation does not state that one should make a copy, as well user expectation is that a method call won't modify the input, i suggest the following FIX: # FIX Just replace the in place modification : with creation of a new array: ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results The assertion should hold: ### Actual Results You know: The assertion fails, because X is changed in place by sklearn code... and thus is now equal to: ### Versions

Title: BayesianRidge prediction standard deviation affected by uniform sample weights
Body: ### Describe the bug The standard deviation of predictions obtained by setting return_std=True on predict(), is clearly affected by uniform sample_weight vectors on fit(). A uniform sample_weight vector will act as a constant on the likelihood function, and I am therefore questioning the effect it has on the standard deviation of predictions. As shown below by the example, the scale (1 vs. 20) of the uniform sample_weight vector directly affects the scale of the standard deviations. Title: Progress bar for BaseSearchCV and its inheritors to track iterative progress more conveniently
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable When , the fitting is shown as a progress bar including the average time/pace and last fit attempted time. ### Describe your proposed solution Implement -like or -like (familiar to epoch tracking) progress bar such that the feature can be enabled through an argument of . This proposal is under the assumption that the iteration is accessible like loop. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context I've been using GridSearchCV and brute-forcing parameters (regarding algorithms, domain-knowledge agnostic) to find the best model parameters while leaving the machine unattended (disconnect from the cloud). Even with tracking, it's hard to skim through the log, especially among hundreds of fits to deduce that the process has been interrupted. The issue expanded from a simple inquiry of #6021 .

Body: Histogram GBDT can segfault if they contain negative categories. Indeed, we documented that they should all be positive in but segfaulting is probably not the best error message. I triggered the problem on my Mac M1 with the following code: It is not obvious that it will always segfault (it did not do that on Linux). I got the following traceback on a similar code snippet in Linux: So I think that it could be safer to check for negative values before to go forward when is passed since applying a marker for unknown categories or rare categories could be something usually done.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Upload a Python wheel for sklearn package
Body: As you know a number of people install scikit-learn via (or specify in their dependencies) which works due to the [ ]( package alias. So far we still have no solution to let users know they shouldn't do this The problem is that if users try to install a package using the Pyodide distribution in their browser the installation will fail because installing .tar.gz archives from PyPi is not supported (and will not be in the near future due to browser constraints) One solution is to upload a pure Python python wheel for the package. This will also make the installation a bit faster for all users who happened to use this alias. The other solution is to try to fix packages that use in their dependencies, but without some centralized mechanism, it's a bit of a hopeless task.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: sklearn implementation of the square-root LASSO
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable The unconstrained LASSO is currently implemented in . However, the parameter for unconstrained LASSO is sensitive to the measurement noise. Instead, the square-root LASSO has objective norm(Ax-b) + alpha * norm(x, 1) and its parameter, alpha, is insensitive to the noise level. I would like an interface (with , , etc.) coupled with a -like function, , which solves the square-root LASSO optimization program. ### Describe your proposed solution I propose an -friendly wrapper around the or interface. More ambitiously, I propose an implementation based on [1] that implements a fast (accelerated?) proximal gradient method for solving square-root LASSO. [1] "On fast convergence of proximal algorithms for SQRT-LASSO optimization: Don’t worry about its nonsmooth loss function" by Li, Xinguo, Jiang, Haoming, et al. (2020) PMLR. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant Currently, offers a square-root LASSO solver implemented via the Python package - this approach is slow. Using with another interior point solver like MOSEK offers only 2x speed-up (it is snails compared to ). Separately, if the problem is appropriately regularized to avoid over-fitting, some work has shown that it's possible to use accelerated proximal gradient descent to achieve convergence. However, the paper offers an algorithm with several critical implementation details unprovided. See [1] above. ### Additional context _No response_

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Reverse Transform and a intelligent skip non existent columns for transformer and pipelines
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable I have almost implemented a wizard that will preprocess the data a user puts in. Than the training of Tensorflow networks will be done and also a integration REST API flow comes out. But here is the catch: We implemented a auto feature preprocessing transformer and wanted to do so with a pipeline. This works very well as long as the input data has not changed or I do not want to apply a reverse transformation. But in my integration flow afterwards this is exactly the case. I have my features but not my targets, so the feature pipeline will not work (except with my workaround: faking the target data and after the transformer pipeline was applied remove the target columns). Okay that was Problemo 1. Here is Problemo numero due: Since in regression use cases the scaled output from the network is not interesting to me, also not the one hot encoded feature vector in classification use cases. So I simply thought just use the pipeline and reverse transform it. But no there is no reverse transform method for pipelines. So heres my next workaround: We scan the pipeline and manually call the reverse transform method on each transformer recursevly. Okay now you are feeling my nausea. To make things even more complicated here also kicks in the columns problem since my predicted data only contains a subset of the previous data columns. Would be lovely when this feature would be implemented. ### Describe your proposed solution Look in text above. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_

Title: sklearn.compose.ColumnTransformer do not keep transformers` desired dtype of output
Body: Hi, We are using ColumnTransformer as our unified preprocessor to transform the data. We have the following transformers: From them we create our ColumnTransformer object: When we use the method the desired dtypes of output for and that should be isn't kept. What we are receiving is numpy's ndarray that its dtype is . We debuged the code, and found out that in order to join the outputs from the different transformers the ColumnTransformer is using the method which creates a unified 1 ndarray with one dtype. Is there a solution? If not: 1. Please add a warning in the documentation of the ColumnTransformer that the concatenate results won't use the desired dtype of output of the transformers. 2. Allow us an option to receive the result without concatenation, as a list of ndarrays.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: API: Simplification of backend class names.
Body: ## Problem The current backend class names are a bit confusing: Current Names The length of the names, especially the further down the hierarchy you go, I think impede the readability of the code as well as make it a bit more confusing to determine the specific roles of everything. For example, it is not obvious to me in the code that and are wildly different, and while the diagram is helpful I don't think it should be as necessary as it is right now. ## Proposed Solution 1 Edit: Changed to per discussion w/ @jjerphan With the recent refactoring into a proper submodule, I think we can greatly simplify the namespace. See proposed naming scheme: Proposed Names This draws a more literal connection between the different classes and makes it easily inferred from the code itself, making the diagram a helpful resource rather than a necessary guide. In general, I think if we follow the current implementation plan of only exposing the dispatchers and implementing each dispatch branch in a separate file (e.g. the branch following lives in ) we could be a bit more aggressive in trimming the names (i.e. no more explicit in name, so ) but I can see why some people may not enjoy that. ## Proposed Solution 2 Edit: I added a bar for just to make it more obvious that there isn't necessarily semantic changes below that point, just overwriting relevant methods with faster, more specialized implementations. Optional, but I think it's nice. I also removed the suffix to demonstrate a more minimal namespace: More Drastic Change cc: @jjerphan

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Unsupervised K-Means Clustering Algorithm
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable "the k-means algorithm and its extensions are always influenced by initializations with a necessary number of clusters a priori. That is, the k-means algorithm is not exactly an unsupervised clustering method. In this paper, we construct an unsupervised learning schema for the k-means algorithm so that it is free of initializations without parameter selection and can also simultaneously find an optimal number of clusters." from the abstract. ### Describe your proposed solution "To construct the k-means clustering algorithm with free of initializations and automatically find the number of clusters, we use the entropy concept. We borrow the idea from the EM algorithm by Yang et al." from section 3 (THE UNSUPERVISED K-MEANS CLUSTERING ALGORITHM). ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant R-EM, C-FS, X-Means, and RL-FCM ### Additional context 309 citations (according to Google Scholar, not sure why IEEE shows a different number), released in 20 April 2020. I think there are up to 5 parameters "γ, β, c, a K, a K," might be wrong. The total computational complexity for the U-k-means algorithm is O(ncd), where n is the number of data points, c is the number of clusters, and d is the dimension of data points. Name of the authors: Kristina P. Sinaga, Miin-Shen Yang

Body: Hi, just was wondering what exactly was meant by the above lines in sklearn's docs for GroupKFold. Based on the examples on [this]( page and the [respective explanation in the User Guide]( each group is used as part of the test subset in just one fold, and for the rest of the folds it's used within the training subset, hence, each group **does** appear in different folds, but it's just never used both within the test subset and the train subset. Would appreciate it if you could clarify this, perhaps, I am missing something. Thanks in advance!

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: AUC differences between cross_validate and cross_val_predict
Body: ### Describe the bug I am working through a binary classification problem and I need to generate an ROC curve. When comparing the performances of the function cross_validate (which returns performance metrics), with cross_val_predict (which can return probabilities), the AUC can vary largely from these two functions when being given the same model and data. This is especially difficult to work with when cross_validate returns much better roc_auc values than cross_val_predict. This has previously been discussed here: ### Steps/Code to Reproduce model = LogisticRegression(class_weight='balanced', penalty='l1', solver='liblinear') scores = cross_validate(clone(model), x, y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc") print(scores) predictions = cross_val_predict(model, x, y, cv=5, method="predict_proba") print(roc_auc_score(y, predictions[:, 1]) ### Expected Results 1 roc_auc 0.727598 0.056468 0. ### Actual Results 1 roc_auc 0.768172 0.056468 0. ### Versions

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Vector Quantization Example increases rather than decreases memory use
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation The [Vector Quantization Example]( doesn't seem to demonstrate Vector Quantization. As written, the kmeans clustering approach used in the example converts a grey scale to an representation ( ). This increases the image memory use by 4x. **Expected output** Vector quantization output demonstrates a decrease in memory use. **Additional details** From [Wikipedia]( "Vector quantization, also called "block quantization" or "pattern matching quantization" is often used in lossy data compression. It works by encoding values from a multidimensional vector space into a finite set of values from a discrete subspace of lower dimension. A lower-space vector requires less storage space, so the data is compressed." I'm guessing kmeans outputs an by default. The cluster are in the range 0,1,2,3,4. While this could be compressed to a 4 bit integer, is as small as we can go with numpy so the example does not effectively illustrate the data compression. Perhaps the tutorial assumption is that the values contained in could be compressed through some other algorithm (e.g. outside of numpy). However, for someone unfamiliar with Vector Quantization, it may seem odd why someone would quantize a vector in a way that both loses information and increases memory use. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix 1. add a comment to clarify that the quantized representation of the original face could be further compressed by another algorithm 2. replace the gray image with a color image and cast the kmeans output to a to demonstrate the compression. Converting 3, 8 bit channels to one 8 bit channel would reduce nbytes by 67%.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: NearestCentroid not handling properly distance metrics other than Manhattan or Euclidean.
Body: ### Describe the bug Three issues, quite related. * Using a metric that is not Manhattan or Euclidean triggers the following warning: , but I believe an error message should be thrown instead. Indeed, computing the centroids as the average of the class samples is wrong for (I think) all distances except Euclidean, so it is misleading to actually do it even with a warning. Throwing an error here would fix the 2 following issues. * Calling on a with metric , or throws an error, because those metrics need params that we can't pass to . #23806 would fix the documentation, but maybe it should be nice to handle this properly in the code. * Using any string as a (even a random string like ) does not throws an error when fitting, but does when calling . Doing this with another classifier like throws an error at the fitting step. If relevant I'd like to work on fixing those issues (new contributor here :)) I noticed a stalled PR (#19262) related to implementing to . Maybe I should start working on it first, as it seems to improve the code quality of , so it would be an interesting base to start with. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results Fitting a with a metric that is not Manhattan or Euclidean should trigger a proper error message. ### Actual Results Error related to the second issue: ### Versions

Title: TfidfVectorizer Argument "Input" Overrides Python Keyword
Body: ### Describe the bug The first argument for the TfidfVectorizer class is named (shown on the documentation page [here]( but this is already the name of a Python keyword. When passing this argument into the class it will be highlighted indicating it is already a Python keyword. This has the potential to cause unintended overrides and should be changed to better follow naming conventions. PEP8 [convention]( is to add a trailing underscore to avoid overrides (e.g. "input_"), but because this variable is a string descriptor it would be more user intuitive to change it to something like "input_type". ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results ### Actual Results ### Versions

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: MeanShift extremely slow when multiple processes are run in parallel
Body: ### Describe the bug I have a script which computes clustering of points using . As a part of my pipeline, I have to run this script many times on different data. The data changes, but the number of points is roughly the same in every file (~700k). When I run the script on 1 CPU it takes approx. 100 seconds. I am not using the in-built parallelism, i.e. I use but I am running many jobs in parallel (either with or directly from bash). I noticed, that my jobs take exceptionally long to run (some took 7000 seconds), even though all of them should take only roughly 100 seconds. I am using a very powerful machine with Dual AMD EPYC 7742 64-Core Processor (i.e. 256 CPUs are available), with 1TB of RAM, and only read intensive NVMe Samsung PM983 drives. I would be very happy if somebody knows a workaround. Thank you for your time. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce File : Create a txt file with for example 1000 jobs: Run the following use cases from the terminal: 1. Running in batches of 1 2. Running in batches of 5 3. Running in batches of 10 4. Running in batches of 50 5. Running in batches of 100 6. Running in batches of 200 ### Expected Results No matter how many processes I spawn, each job should finish in approx. the same time. In the example case above, on my aforementioned machine, it should take ~40 seconds per job. Please note, no other exhaustive processes are running on the machine and most of the RAM is free. ### Actual Results When running only as much as 50 jobs in parallel (on a 256 core machine), some jobs take 10x longer. With increasing number of parallel processes, the situation gets worse. With 100 processes, the worst case is 20x longer. With 200 processes, the worst case is almost 30x longer. Better overview in the plot bellow. Also, please note that even when the process takes so abnormally long to finish, it still appears as *running* in htop and uses 99% of CPU, so it is not sleeping. ![image]( ### Versions

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: KMeans not working with multiprocessing
Body: TL;DR: it seems that kmeans need to somehow release processors after running, otherwise multiprocessing package cannot call it properly. I found this issue when I tried to build a multiprocessing tool that iteratively calls KMeans. It works fine if KMeans is only executed in the child processes. However, if the parent process executes KMeans, the child processes cannot execute KMeans anymore. To show this is the case, I did the test in 3 scenarios: 1) I only called KMeans in the child processes, and it worked fine; 2) I called KMeans in both the parent and the child processes, only the parent process would work and all child processes timed out; 3) I called KMeans by specifying the number of processors each time, then all processes would work. I have demonstrated it using a Notebook here:

Title: Unexpected interaction between param validation and _get_tags in TweedieRegressor
Body: Calling _get_tags on a TweedieRegressor with invalid value for the link or power parameter raises an error. This is because the tag "requires_positive_y" is computed dynamically and depends on these 2 parameters. It causes this CI failure which happened after merging which adds param validation for GLMs and which makes the common test for param validation rely on the tags. It brings 2 questions: - should _more_tags in Tweedie perform validation for these 2 parameters to raise an informative error, even if it's a validation before fit ? - would it be fine to implement an alternative to that does not rely on the tags, a bit like @glemaitre's first implementation ? cc/ @glemaitre @thomasjpfan

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: allow sklearn pipeline cache to ignore certain arguments
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable To the best of my understanding, sklearn pipeline cache helps reduce computation when the full argument list matches what has been cached. The main motivation of caching seems to cache the transformer steps, and transformer often only depends on X, not y. However, the current caching considers both X and y together to decide whether to compute again. For example, the transformer could transform both X and y separately as a function of some parameter to be searched over using GridSearchCV. Repeated transformation of X for different y could be very expensive. Another scenario is that there are different ways to construct X given some parameter to be searched over using GridSearchCV. A pipeline can have a transformer to achieve this as the first step. During pipeline.fit(), the transformer.fit() will ignore X and y and create actual data, and transformer.transform(X) can accept X as a row index to select a subset from the created data as output to be used in the following steps of the pipeline. In the following code, it shows that pipeline.fit() is computed for all the input pairs, because y is different. (It also seems that currently the internal state of the pipeline will decide whether to compute again or not) It will be great if there could be an ignore argument when initializing Pipeline, which can allow the reuse of some previous transformer fit (and possibly the option to ignore certain internal states/attributes if necessary). I have searched for solutions, and tried different ideas. For example, if the cache can apply to certain steps of a pipeline, that would serve the purpose as well, but I didn't find such an option. I thought of having a pipeline inside another pipeline, and cache the inside one, but the input argument from the outer to the inner pipeline will give rise to the same issue. Maybe there is already a way to allow flexible data creation given a parameter to be searched over in GridSearchCV that I do not know of. If so, I would appreciate it if you point me to the reference. Thank you very much! ### Describe your proposed solution It seems that joblib.Memory.cache has an ignore argument, which allows ignoring certain arguments in decorating the function being cached. If we could allow the user to specify which arguments in the pipeline.fit() when creating the pipeline, that would make pipeline more flexible. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler() needs a degrees of freedom tunable parameter for scale_
Body: ### Describe the workflow you want to enable I'm scaling a dataset using the standard procedure. I'm comparing the results I get with the results from the R function . They are subtly different. Tracking down the difference, it turns out sklearn uses a different definition for the standard deviation, one that differs from R in the degrees of freedom assumed. sklearn assumes ddof=0, while R assumes ddof=1. I do not wish to debate which approach is "more correct". I am fine with sklearn continuing with the current approach as the default. I only want to suggest that, for those who think the slight DoF correction is necessary, should provide an option to tune the degrees of freedom, either in the initial call, or later in the call, or wherever it is deemed more appropriate. ### Describe your proposed solution Basically, leave the default as-is. Introduce a new parameter, let's call it , with a default value of 0, but allow the user to specify a different value for it if they need it. Or have a boolean switch, by default False, which when True enables the Bessel correction, which is the same as using N-1 instead of N for the standard deviation. I do not know enough about the sklearn internals to judge which solution is better. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant This workaround forces to use ddof=1, but it's an extra step and it computes the STD twice. With large datasets, that would slow down the computation and it's wasteful. ### Additional context

Title: TSNE is broken in pyodide
Body: While testing and thus , it seems that is broken. The reason is that we are specifically using 32 bits internally that do not work with . I think that we should make sure that preserve dtype in 64 bits and 32 bits without silently reducing the numerical precision. The current workaround is to use and externalize the initialization and pass a 64 bits NumPy array. Solely this case preserves the computation in 64 bits.

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Fix broken links in the documentation
Body: Below is the list of broken links in the documention from a run, together with the file the link appears in and the error message. **If you want to work on this**, please: - do **one Pull Request per link** - **add a comment in this issue saying which link you want to tackle** so that different people can work on this issue in parallel - **mention this issue ( ) in your Pull Request description** so that progress on this issue can more easily be tracked Possible solutions for a broken link include: - find a replacement for the broken link. In case of links to articles, being able to link to a resource where the article is openly accessible (rather than behind a paywall) would be nice. - The link can be added to the variable: This is the only thing to do for example when: + the link is broken with no replacement (for example in testimonials some companies were acquired and their website does not exist) + the link works fine in a browser but is flagged as broken by tool. This may happen because some websites are trying to prevent bots to scrape the content of their website Something that may be useful in the complicated cases is to search on the [Internet Archive]( for the broken link. You may be able to look at the old content and it may help you to find an appropriate link replacement. - [x] modules/generated/sklearn.linear_model.OrthogonalMatchingPursuit.rst - [x] modules/decomposition.rst - [x] modules/generated/sklearn.datasets.make_swiss_roll.rst - [x] modules/linear_model.rst - [x] #23660 modules/linear_model.rst - [x] datasets/real_world.rst - [x] modules/decomposition.rst - [x] auto_examples/neighbors/plot_species_kde.rst - [x] modules/outlier_detection.rst - [x] modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.hinge_loss.rst - [x] modules/decomposition.rst - [x] modules/kernel_approximation.rst - [x] modules/linear_model.rst - [x] modules/decomposition.rst - [x] developers/bug_triaging.rst - [x] developers/contributing.rst - [x] #23697 modules/grid_search.rst - [x] #23739 modules/generated/sklearn.cluster.spectral_clustering.rst - [x] datasets/loading_other_datasets.rst - [x] presentations.rst - [x] modules/compose.rst - [x] presentations.rst - [x] modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.d2_absolute_error_score.rst - [x] modules/generated/sklearn.random_projection.SparseRandomProjection.rst - [x] modules/generated/sklearn.decomposition.IncrementalPCA.rst - [x] about.rst - [x] about.rst - [x] modules/generated/sklearn.impute.IterativeImputer.rst - [x] This link is working in a browser, it should be addded to similarly to what was done in #23737 modules/svm.rst - [x] about.rst - [x] modules/clustering.rst - [x] This link is working in a browser, it should be addded to similarly to what was done in #23737 modules/generated/sklearn.datasets.load_boston.rst - [x] modules/generated/sklearn.linear_model.RANSACRegressor.rst - [x] modules/cross_decomposition.rst

input:
Title: sklearn.utils._param_validation._InstancesOf is insufficient for numpy data types
Body: ### Describe the bug Numpy data types can be constructed in different ways. Although they result in the same data type, isinstance() yields different results based on the way the data type has been constructed. Therefore, if an object accepts numpy data types as a parameter, the parameter constraints need to be . Currently this isn't supported, because initalizes with the built-in This results in errors when trying to implement parameter_constraints in objects with numpy data types as parameters. E.g. [#23579]( I therefore propose to change the parameter self.type to self.param_type and initialize it with the actual type given within the list of parameter constraints. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce See ### Expected Results No error is thrown. ### Actual Results ### Versions

Title: Confusion regarding BinomialDeviance
Body: ### Describe the issue linked to the documentation The [BinomialDeviance]( states that it computes the and then it proceeds by computing the following functions Let's take the first case in the if statement, , which seems to be computing . Shouldn't that instead be $y * \log(p) + (1-y) \log(1-p)$ where $p$ is a vector of probabilities obtained from when $y = 2$ classes? Another thing that I don't fully follow is the computation in the respective function which computes the following gradient . From the comment it states that it computes the gradient and then multiplies it by 1/2, . Is that correct? Shouldn't the gradient of with respect to the be $y - \frac{e^{raw}}{1+e^{raw}}$ where refers to the ? The expression $y - \frac{e^{raw}}{1+e^{raw}}$ can be simplified into $y - \sigma(raw)(1-\sigma(raw))$ where $\sigma$ is the logistic function. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix _No response_

instruction: Classify the following GitHub issue based on its title and body.
input:
Title: Wrong order for check_sample_weights_invariance
Body: ### Describe the bug The intent of check_sample_weights_invariance here: is as it says that for kind="zeros", will produce a y1 such that a y2 with weights will give be "identical". However, this is not true. Instead, the rows gets shuffled. This matters for any method that relies upon row-order, even if reproducible. E.g. tree methods that do row sampling will be affected. Any splitting that occurs in the estimator itself will be different due to the different order. Any batching for neural networks will learn in a different order. etc. The code should not shuffle the output for y2. The shuffle line should be removed. ### Steps/Code to Reproduce ### Expected Results No error in above copy-paste from the function with just different setup and check added at end. No shuffle should occur. ### Actual Results gives: i.e. *not* identical. ### Versions `
